title: Heavy Metal Concentrations in SelectedHerbal Drinks Sold in Abeokuta, Ogun State,and Their Toxicological Risk Assessment

paper_analysis.summary: This paper measures potentially toxic metals (Cd, Cu, Fe, Mn, Pb) in 10 commercially sold herbal drinks from Abeokuta, Nigeria using acid digestion followed by atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS). Cd, Fe, and Mn exceeded WHO limits in subsets of samples, while Pb was not detected and Cu was below the WHO limit in all samples; human health risk assessment via average daily intake and hazard quotient indicated non-carcinogenic risk below 1 for adults and children.
measurement_types: acid digestion of liquid beverage samples
atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) for metal concentration
human health risk assessment calculations (average daily intake, hazard quotient)
comparative normalization to WHO guideline values

figures.description: Map of Abeokuta City extracted from Ogun State/Nigeria, showing the study area where herbal drink samples were collected.
figures.likely_source: experimental/field context image compiled from geographic map data

figures.description: Comparative study of heavy metal concentrations normalized to WHO standards across the 10 herbal drink samples; highlights large Fe/WHO and Mn/WHO ratios in Otoge and fluctuations across samples.
Percentage hazard quotient of each heavy metal for adult and children populations, showing non-carcinogenic risk contributions by metal.
figures.likely_source: processed experimental data with calculated risk metrics

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: Raw measurements consist of triplicate AAS readings of Cd, Cu, Fe, Mn, and Pb concentrations in 10 herbal drink samples after nitric-acid digestion of 20 mL aliquots. The paper does not provide instrument output files, spectra, or downloadable raw datasets; only summarized concentrations (mean ± SD) are reported.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: No explicit data availability statement, repository link, downloadable dataset, or supplementary data archive is mentioned in the provided text.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: Raw data are implied to be AAS measurements from digested beverage samples, taken in triplicate, but the paper does not describe file formats, exported instrument files, or provide the underlying point-by-point instrument outputs.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data are presented as mean ± standard deviation concentrations (mg/L) for each metal in each sample in Table 1, along with WHO comparison values. Further processed outputs include average daily intake values (Table 3), hazard quotients (Table 4), and comparative normalized values used for Fig. 2 and Fig. 3.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Figure-specific source data are not explicitly provided. Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 appear to be generated from the tabulated concentration and risk-assessment results, but no separate figure data files are mentioned.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: No scripts, notebooks, or code are mentioned. Data analysis, graphical presentation, and toxicological risk calculations were performed in Microsoft Excel 2013.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset is small and sample-centric: 10 herbal drink brands/samples labeled A–J, each measured for five metals. The paper distinguishes raw analytical measurement (AAS after digestion) from processed summary tables and derived risk metrics. Pb is reported as not detected in all samples. Triplicate measurements are explicitly stated, and significance is reported as P>0.05, but no inferential statistics workflow or raw replicate values are shown.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The methods state that 20 mL of each herbal sample was digested and analyzed by AAS, and Table 1 reports mean ± SD of triplicate measurements, implying underlying replicate raw measurements.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: Tables 1, 2, 3, and 4 provide processed concentrations and derived risk metrics; Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 are figure-ready summaries derived from these processed values.
classification_prior.data_availability_section_relevant: “Mean±Standard Deviation (SD) of triplicate measurements.” “The Microsoft Excel 2013 version was utilized for data analysis, graphical presentation, and also for computing the toxicological risk assessment.”
